coredata.dc:title: Boosting photocatalytic activity through in-situ phase transformation of bismuth-based compounds on carbon dots and quantification analysis of intrinsically reactive species in photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhaoqi
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Huiqi
Li, Ning
Liu, Hantao
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: As a photocatalyst, both BiOCOOH and (BiO)2CO3 are only active under UV light irradiation. In this contribution, a room-temperature approach is firstly demonstrated to develop in situ incorporation of green carbon dots (CDs) into BiOCOOH (CDs@BiOCOOH) by taking advantage of the residual HCOOH for preparing CDs. As-prepared CDs@BiOCOOH is endowed with broad visible light absorption. By the reaction with NaBH4, BiOCOOH is subsequently transformed into (BiO)2CO3 at room temperature, forming a new heterostructure of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. Not only does this heterostructure display exceptionally stronger visible light harvesting capability than CDs@BiOCOOH due to the introduction of more structural defects in the process of phase transformation, but it also possesses more superior degradation ability for the organic pollutants under visible light. Its photocatalytic activity is raised to over 7 times comparing to BiOCOOH. Accordingly, a facile and room-temperature synthesis strategy is presented for large-scale production of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 with highly efficient use of sunlight. To understand photocatalytic mechanism, we offer a unique technique for the quantification and the kinetics evaluation of the intrinsic reactive species produced in photocatalysis as well.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterostructure
Photocatalysis
Carbon dots
Bismuth-based compounds

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Zeta potential values of CDs, BiOCOOH and CDs@ BiOCOOH; (b) XRD patterns of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3; (c) XPS survey spectra and (d) Bi 4f, (e) O 1s, (f) C 1s spectra of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM and EDX element mapping images indicated by the red frame for CDs@BiOCOOH (a) and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 (b).
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of CDs@BiOCOOH; (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) N2 sorption isotherm and (b) pore size distributions of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3; the inset in (b) showing a large magnification indicated by the dot frame. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and degradation rates of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for RhB (b), MB (c) and MO (d) under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Trapping experiments of reactive species during the catalytic degradation of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for RhB (a), MB (b), and MO (c) under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Time dependence of fluorescence intensity at 425 nm with BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3, respectively (a) and absorption spectra of NBT solution over BiOCOOH (b), CDs@BiOCOOH (c) and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 (d) after different irradiation time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − signals, (a) and (c): pure BiOCOOH, (b) and (d): CDs@(BiO)2CO3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of a series of solution with the known TAOH concentrations; (b) Linear dependence of the fluorescence intensity of TAOH on its concentrations; (c) Plots of nonlinear fitting of Michaelis-Menten curves for TA catalyzed by BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 under visible light irradiation; (d–f) Comparison plots of the V max and photodegradation rates for RhB, MB and MO dyes in the presence of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Valence-band XPS spectra of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3; (b) EIS spectra and (c) Transient photocurrent responses of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH, (BiO)2CO3 and CDs@(BiO)2CO3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The recyclability of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for RhB photodegradation at the same condition; (b) The effect of the light intensity on the degradation rate of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for RhB. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation processes for CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3.

head.ce:title: Boosting photocatalytic activity through in-situ phase transformation of bismuth-based compounds on carbon dots and quantification analysis of intrinsically reactive species in photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan, 030051, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, P. R. China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a photocatalyst, both BiOCOOH and (BiO)2CO3 are only active under UV light irradiation. In this contribution, a room-temperature approach is firstly demonstrated to develop in situ incorporation of green carbon dots (CDs) into BiOCOOH (CDs@BiOCOOH) by taking advantage of the residual HCOOH for preparing CDs. As-prepared CDs@BiOCOOH is endowed with broad visible light absorption. By the reaction with NaBH4, BiOCOOH is subsequently transformed into (BiO)2CO3 at room temperature, forming a new heterostructure of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. Not only does this heterostructure display exceptionally stronger visible light harvesting capability than CDs@BiOCOOH due to the introduction of more structural defects in the process of phase transformation, but it also possesses more superior degradation ability for the organic pollutants under visible light. Its photocatalytic activity is raised to over 7 times comparing to BiOCOOH. Accordingly, a facile and room-temperature synthesis strategy is presented for large-scale production of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 with highly efficient use of sunlight. To understand photocatalytic mechanism, we offer a unique technique for the quantification and the kinetics evaluation of the intrinsic reactive species produced in photocatalysis as well.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on photoredox and photocatalysis has been inspired in part by the realization that sunlight is effectively an inexhaustible energy source [1–3]. Nevertheless, the utilization of visible light in photocatalysis is fundamentally challenging because single-component photocatalysts tend not to have wide light-absorption range and strong redox ability [3–5]. Recent study has begun to design proper heterogeneous photocatalytic systems to overcome aforementioned problems [5–11]. These systems could be simultaneously endowed with broad visible light-absorption range and the high charge-separation efficiency [6,8–10]. While useful, there is a striking short of strategy to enhance the intrinsic activity of photocatalysts, which mainly originates from the chemical composition and structure of the catalyst material instead of its geometrical structuring and heterogeneous interface [5,12]. Since the intrinsic activity of a photocatalyst determines the reactivity and reaction kinetics in catalysis, more active sites are needed to be created in catalyst material for improving the photocatalytic performance.

The Sillén phase compounds usually possess layered structures, with alternating (BiO)2 2+ sheets and X layers (X = halogen, COOH, CO3 2−, or other ion group) [13–18]. Currently, some advances have been made in the controlled preparation of the sillén structure BiOX with different morphologies and their uses for the photodegradation of organic pollutants [7,13–17]. Among these bismuth-based semiconductors, some compounds such as BiOCOOH and (BiO)2CO3, are only responsive under UV light [14–17,19,20], limiting their utilization in the conversion of solar energy. To exploit them working under visible light, it has been developed to modify their wide band gaps by the doping and hybrid technology [16,17,19–21]. Both BiOCOOH and (BiO)2CO3 belong to Sillén phase compounds, but have different anion groups in alternative stacking of (BiO)2 2+ layers. The in situ transformation of BiOCOOH to (BiO)2CO3 could produce abundant defects (e.g. oxygen vacancies and dangling bonds) due to layered structure changes. The insertion of the catalytically relevant defects into layered structure of Sillén phase compounds is expected to not only engineer their band gaps, but also provide more intrinsically active sites for them [18,22,23]. Unfortunately, there have been no reports of related work by far.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) not only are low cost, facile preparation and low environmental hazard, but also possess broad light-harvesting range, tunable edge defects, superior electron-transfer efficiency, and good photoinduced redox properties [24–26]. Therefore, they have shown indispensable and fascinating prospects in the solar energy conversion [24–26]. Recently, we have developed an approach to prepare CDs by chemical etching of coal pitch powder with a mixed solution of HCOOH and H2O2 at room temperature [27]. To make full use of residual HCOOH in the suspension of CDs, we planned to fabricate BiOCOOH in the presence of CDs and obtain the heterostructure of CDs and BiOCOOH (namely CDs@BiOCOOH). According to the latest report, however, CDs@BiOCOOH only shows the limited enhancement in visible-light harvesting ability and photocatalytic activity [20]. Accordingly, we proposed a novel strategy to reconstruct this heterostructure, i.e. the in situ phase transformation of BiOCOOH into (BiO)2CO3 on CDs gives rise to the formation of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. This novel strategy enables more catalytically relevant defects to be generated possibly in (BiO)2CO3, rendering remarkably enhanced activity of visible-light-response catalysts [18,23]. In particular, all of preparation and chemical reaction were completed at room temperature without any external heating, thus in favor of energy conservation in the process of material preparation. For better standardized evaluation of photocatalytic activity, we furthermore offered a method towards quantification of measurement for intrinsically reactive species in photocatalysis. It can clearly verify the degree of activity differences among photocatalysts and understand the origin of photocatalytic reaction.

The preparation procedure of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 are shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the suspension of CDs was prepared by the mixed solution of HCOOH and H2O2 as reported previously [27]. Typically, 200 mg of coal pitch powder was dispersed in the mixed solution of 30 mL HCOOH (88 v/v%) and 3 mL H2O2 (30 v/v%). After continuously stirring for 20 h at room temperature, the suspension of CDs was obtained by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. Secondly, 500 mg of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O powder was added in the resultant suspension of CDs. At room temperature, the obtained slurry reacted for 10 h under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the precipitates were collected and then washed with distilled water. After dried by a vacuum at 60 °C for 2 h, the gray powders of CDs@BiOCOOH (Fig. S1a) were obtained finally.

The CDs@(BiO)2CO3 was prepared using as-prepared CDs@BiOCOOH powder. Typically, 50 mg of CDs@BiOCOOH was dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water under vigorously stirring. Subsequently, 5 mL of NaBH4 solution (1 g L−1) was added into the obtained suspension drop by drop. After reacting for 30 min at room temperature, the precipitates were further obtained by centrifugation at 5000 rpm. At last, the precipitates were washed with distilled water for several times and dried at 60 °C for 2 h to obtain the gray-blue powders (Fig. S1b), i.e. CDs@(BiO)2CO3.

For comparison, the sample of BiOCOOH was also prepared by the reaction of HCOOH with Bi(NO3)3·5H2O at room temperature. In detail, 5 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was put into a beaker, followed with 10 mL of formic acid solution (88 v/v%) added drop by drop. When the reaction was ended, the white precipitates were collected by centrifugation and then was thoroughly washed with distilled water several times and dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 2 h. Finally, the white powders of BiOCOOH (Fig. S1c) were obtained.

Since as-prepared CDs were obtained by chemical etching of coal pitch powder with a mixed solution of HCOOH and H2O2, expectedly the surface of CDs contained abundant carboxylic acid groups and adsorbed HCOOH molecules [27]. Thermodynamically, this case allows reduction of the interface energy between the CDs and the nuclei of BiOCOOH, thus promotes the heterogeneous nucleation of BiOCOOH on the surface of CDs [8,28,29]. Moreover, we also measured the zeta potentials of CDs and BiOCOOH and the measured results were given in Fig. 1 a. Clearly, their potentials are opposite, implying that they are prone to mutual attraction. So it is easy to form the heterostructure of CDs@BiOCOOH in the presence of CDs during the nucleation of BiOCOOH. To verify it, the prepared sample was examined using the powder XRD patterns (Fig. 1b). Its distinct diffraction peaks can be assigned to the tetragonal BiOCOOH (JCPDS card 35–0939) while the peak at 23° belongs to graphite diffraction peaks of CDs [13,16,20]. After it reacted with NaBH4, however, a new set of diffraction peaks were observed while the corresponding diffraction peaks of BiOCOOH disappeared in XRD pattern (Fig. 1b). The new presented diffraction peaks are in accordance with the standard diffraction pattern of (BiO)2CO3 (JCPDS card 41–1488) [14,15]. This certifies the transformation of BiOCOOH to (BiO)2CO3 in the presented conditions.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to identify surface compositions and element valence states in CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 (Fig. 1c–f). The general peak shapes in XPS survey spectra were unchanged after the reaction of CDs@BiOCOOH with NaBH4 and similar elemental compositions of Bi, C, and O were observed. The high-resolution XPS Bi 4f spectrum of CDs@BiOCOOH displays two symmetrical characteristic peaks at ca. 159.38 and 164.68 eV ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 orbitals, respectively [20]. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the Bi 4f signal is shifted to lower binding energy after the reaction of CDs@BiOCOOH with NaBH4. This shift of Bi 4f signal is probably induced by Bi-related structural defects and/or dangling bonds formed in CDs@(BiO)2CO3. Meanwhile, their O 1s spectra show different oxygen species as well (Fig. 1e). The O 1s signal can be deconvoluted into CO/CO3 2− (or the lattice oxygen) and C–O species located at 529.78 and 531.18 eV, respectively. From Fig. 1e, the O signal is also shifted and the content of CO/CO3 2− species increase remarkably with the transformation of CDs@BiOCOOH to CDs@(BiO)2CO3. What’s more, this result is verified by the C 1s spectra, in which the peak of CO in CDs@(BiO)2CO3becomes stronger than that in CDs@BiOCOOH.

The morphology of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs@BiOCOOH was assembled by thin and large nanosheets and Bi, O, and C atoms were distributed evenly over its entire nanostructure from energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) element mapping. After it reacted with NaBH4, the thin and large nanosheets were destroyed and turned to thick and small nanosheets (Fig. 2b). The more uniform distribution of Bi, O, and C atoms was further observed from the corresponding EDX element mapping. Fig. 3 shows typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of as-prepared CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3. Many dark dots with several nanometer diameters are attached on the surface of nanosheets in as-prepared CDs@BiOCOOH (Fig. 3a). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was further employed to determine the crystal structure of CDs@BiOCOOH (Fig. 3b). HRTEM image depicts two kinds of lattice fringes. The fringe spacing of 0.271 nm is associated to the (110) planes of the orthorhombic BiOCOOH while the fringe spacing of 0.22 nm is consistent with the (100) in-plane lattice of graphite [13]. Typically, TEM image of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 displays many pores (labeled by red arrow) in its layer-like structure and no distinct dark dots as with that of CDs@BiOCOOH (Fig. 3c). This could be attributed to the transformation of CDs@BiOCOOH to CDs@(BiO)2CO3 via the reaction with NaBH4. Not only were many structure defects produced, but also the CDs attached on BiOCOOH were covered by newly formed (BiO)2CO3. By HRTEM image of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 (Fig. 3d), besides the pores resulted from point defects the abundant stacking faults (located by red circles) are also observed owing to its forming process of layer-by-layer stacking.

To certify the above TEM results, we further conducted the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms as well as pore size distributions of CDs@BiOCOOH before and after the reaction with NaBH4 (Fig. 4 a). The specific surface areas of CDs@BiOCOOH before and after the reaction with NaBH4 are calculated to be 75.82 and 99.15 m2 g−1, respectively. The comparatively large surface area of CDs@BiOCOOH could be attributed to nanoscale morphology with mainly textural (interparticle) porosity. By the transformation of CDs@BiOCOOH to CDs@(BiO)2CO3, the larger specific surface area is yielded and is probably derived from structural defects induced by in situ transformation reaction. In addition, the pore size distribution calculated by the Broekhoff de Boer (BdB) spherical model principally ranges from 1 to 6 nm for both samples (Fig. 4b), implying the coexistence of micropores and mesopores in them. Nevertheless, CDs@(BiO)2CO3 exhibits the larger micropore volume and smaller micropore sizes in contrast with CDs@BiOCOOH. As for this phenomenon, the structural defects generated in CDs@(BiO)2CO3 could make a tremendous contribution to more new micropore formation.

Fig. 5 a provides ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of pure BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3. The white coloured BiOCOOH can only absorb ultraviolet light with a wavelength shorter than ∼360 nm while the incorporation of CDs into BiOCOOH (i.e. CDs@BiOCOOH) remarkably improves absorption of visible light wavelengths. Although this CDs@BiOCOOH displays a rapid decrease in visible light absorption as the wavelength increases, its absorption wavelength can still extend the entire visible region range. In contrast, the hydrothermal-sintering prepared products with the highest activity can solely absorb visible wavelengths below 550 nm [20]. As a result, our presented room-temperature route is better than hydrothermal-sintering method for extending absorption wavelengths of BiOCOOH. After the transformation of CDs@BiOCOOH into CDs@(BiO)2CO3, the unexpectedly stronger and broader absorption in the entire visible light region is observed clearly from the UV–Vis spectrum of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. This demonstrates that the CDs@(BiO)2CO3 possesses the more superior capability for solar energy harvesting than CDs@BiOCOOH.

The disposal of organic pollutants such as textile dyes from industry into water is highly toxic to aquatic life and brings serious environmental contamination [1,4]. Methyl orange (MO), rhodamine-B (RhB), and methylene blue (MB) are three representative dyes and they are fairly stable under visible light irradiation in the absence of photocatalysts [1]. So they were often used as model contaminants to assess the visible-light activity of a photocatalyst. Here, we investigated their degradation in water under visible light irradiation using BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 as the photocatalyst, respectively. The photocatalytic activities of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 (Figs. S2–S4) were evaluated by measuring the photodegradation rates of three model pollutants in water irradiated by visible light (xenon lamp with a filter at λ > 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 5b–d, almost no visible-light activity is observed from the pure BiOCOOH for three model pollutants. After the incorporation of CDs into BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH is endowed with the visible light absorption property and so it presents a good visible-light activity. Its photodegradation rates from quantitative evaluation of the first-order degradation rate constant [30], k = ln (C/C0)/t reaches to 0.043 min−1, 0.059 min−1, and 0.128 min−1 for RhB, MB and MO, respectively. Most interestingly, CDs@(BiO)2CO3 exhibits much higher photocatalytic activities in photodegradation of RhB and MB dyes under visible light irradiation. The obtained degradation rates for RhB (0.086 min−1) and MB (0.118 min−1) increase over 200% comparing to CDs@BiOCOOH. Although CDs@(BiO)2CO3 displays relatively high photocatalytic activity for MO degradation as well, unexpectedly, its degradation rate is very close to that of CDs@BiOCOOH (Fig. 5d).

It is well-known that photocatalysis is realized by the redox reactions caused by photoinduced electrons (e−) and holes (h+) on the surfaces of photocatalysts [2]. By the reactions with the holes and electrons, oxygen and water as reaction species can be converted into reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as superoxide anion radical (•O2−) and hydroxyl radical (•OH), which are considered to be primary intermediates of photocatalytic reactions and can participate in the actual redox reactions in photocatalysis [31]. Accordingly, the identification, the quantification, and the kinetics evaluation of these reactive species from photocatalytic reaction are necessary to understand the photodegradation mechanisms [31].

First of all, the different scavengers were used to identify the roles of reactive species in the photodegradation of three model pollutants when CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 act as the photocatalysts. As reported by the literature [7,10,32], we also chose Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) as the h+ scavenger, Isopropanol (IPA) as the •OH scavengers, and benzoquinone (BQ) as the •O2 − radical scavenger. Fig. 6 clearly reveals the rapid degradation of three model pollutants without the addition of scavengers under visible light. Interestingly, the addition of EDTA-2Na can inhibit the photodegradation of three model pollutants significantly, but this case does not work while the IPA and BQ as scavengers. Although the IPA can inhibit the photodegradation of RhB and MB, its role is deactivated for MO. Instead, the BQ can only prevent the degradation of MO under visible light. These results indicate that the photoinduced holes promote degradation of three model pollutants through direct charge transfer process while the •OH and •O2 − radicals generated by the reactions with the holes and electrons show a particular selectivity for dye degradation. To confirm above phenomena, the detection of •OH and •O2 − radicals was further conducted for our presented photocatalytic system.

Terephthalic acid (TA) reacts with •OH to form highly fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH), the concentration of which can be determined by fluorescence intensity [10,11,31,33]. This reaction offers an efficient and sensitive method for identifying and monitoring the •OH radical production. Under the same reaction conditions, we measured fluorescence spectra from the TA solution containing as-prepared photocatalysts for different irradiation time (Fig. S5). For comparison, Fig. 7 a depicts the dependence of fluorescence intensity on irradiation time. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity increases linearly with the irradiation time. The production rates of TAOH calculated from the slopes demonstrate that the amount of •OH radicals generated by CDs@(BiO)2CO3 is over 8.6 and 2.1 times higher than that from BiOCOOH and CDs@BiOCOOH, respectively, under the same conditions. On the other hand, the yellow nitro blue tetrazorium (NBT) can be reduced by •O2 − radicals to generate blue formazan [9,10]. Hence this reaction can be also utilized to detect •O2− radical production by monitoring characteristic absorption peak of NBT. Fig. 7b shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NBT alkaline solution containing BiOCOOH for different irradiation time. Under visible light, the characteristic absorption peak of NBT has a little decrease with irradiation time. By contrast, both CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 enable more •O2− radical production and convert much more NBT into formazan, leading to the rapid decrease of characteristic peak absorption of NBT with irradiation time (Fig. 7c and d). To verify the above detection method, moreover, we used spin-trapping electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR) technique to determine the generation of •OH and •O2 − radicals with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in water and methanol, respectively [20,31,32]. Fig. 8 reveals no clear ESR signals for BiOCOOH with or without visible-light irradiation. As expected, the markedly enhanced ESR signals of •OH and •O2 − radicals are presented in CDs@(BiO)2CO3, indicating the production of both abundant •OH and •O2 − species in this system. This confirms that ESR observations are in well agreement with the detection results obtained by monitoring fluorescence emission and optical absorption mentioned above (Fig. 7).

Although the above detection methods of the intrinsic reactive species have been widely employed to identify the photocatalytic activities or understand the photocatalytic mechanism of photocatalysts, they are still lack of use cases of the quantification and the kinetics evaluation for these reactive species so far. Assuming the TAOH generation through the reaction of one TA molecule with one •OH radical, thus the concentration of TAOH is equal to that of •OH radicals produced in the photocatalytic reaction system. However, some factors such as the initial concentration of TA, the reaction time and the used amount of photocatalysts, can influence the formation of TAOH, resulting in deviation from the exact results. Based on Michaelis-Menten model [10,34], here we described the production rate of TAOH as a function of TA concentration. The obtained maximal production rate of TAOH is in well accordance with the degradation rates of the photocatalysts for dyes.

In detail, we firstly measured fluorescence emission spectra of a series of solution with the known TAOH concentrations (Fig. 9 a). Subsequently, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of TAOH and its concentration was established as shown in Fig. 9b and obtained a fitted equation: Y = 0.13547X. By this equation, one can determine the TAOH concentration generated in real photocatalytic reaction system within its suitable range. Under steady-state conditions, the changes of TAOH production rates with TA concentrations were examined in the presence of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3, respectively (Fig. S6). A series of production rates of TAOH (v) are then calculated and applied to
v =
V max
[ S ]
/
(
[ S ]
+
[
K m
]
)
[10,35], where [S] is the TA concentration, K
m is the Michaelis−Menten constant, and V max is the maximal production velocity of TAOH. As Fig. 8c reveals, typical Michaelis–Menten curves were observed for three samples. The fitted parameter V max is 3.92, 14.33, and 29.68 nM min−1 for BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3, respectively. Since the V max defines the photocatalyst activity [34], it can be deduced on the basis of the V max values that the photodegradation performance of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for dyes is 7.6 and 2.1 times higher than that of BiOCOOH and CDs@BiOCOOH, respectively. To illustrate this method reliability, we conducted the comparisons between the V max values of BiOCOOH, CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 and their corresponding degradation rates for dyes under visible light irradiation. Based on the results shown in Fig. 6, the •OH radicals and the holes are active species of RhB and MB degradation. However, they are actually equal to one kind of active species since the •OH radicals can be formed by the rapid reaction of the holes with H2O and/or –OH. As illustrated in Fig. 9d and e, the V max values of three samples are well matching with their photodegradation rates for RhB and MB dyes, i.e. both the plotted curves can be overlapped. For MO degradation, the •O2− radicals and the holes are active species and they can’t transform into each other. On the other hand, CDs@BiOCOOH has a good ability to yield •O2− radicals under visible light as shown in Fig. 7c. As a result, Fig. 9f displays a non-linear dependence of photodegradation rates on the V max. By above results and discussion, it can be found that our presented method shows the high reliability and high sensitivity for catalytic activity quantification and the evaluation of catalytic reaction kinetics.

The roles of CDs played in as-prepared photocatalysts are crucial. As described above, the incorporation of CDs into BiOCOOH and (BiO)2CO3 greatly enhance visible-light capture and utilization. This phenomenon, on the one hand, can be attributed to the introduction of new energy levels from CDs; on the other hand, the energy band positions are adjusted probably due to the effect of heterogeneous interfaces induced by CDs. By XPS valence spectra [7,20], we further ascertained the influences of CDs and phase transformation on valence band (VB) positions (Fig. 10 a). It can be found that the VB maximum (VBM) position of BiOCOOH reduces after carbon dot incorporation. Similarly, the VBM position of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 is shifted in contrast with CDs@BiOCOOH, and it reaches to 2.05 eV, which approximates to the oxidation potential of –OH, E (•OH/−OH)pH=7 = 1.99 eV) [32]. Thus, the photoinduced holes may quickly react with –OH to generate •OH, relieving other ways of their consumption. As a consequent, more •OH radicals are able to be produced from CDs@(BiO)2CO3 than the other samples, then resulting in highly efficient catalysis under visible light.

Furthermore, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique was utilized to analyze the effects of CDs on interfacial carrier transfer behaviors [8,10,11]. The smaller of the semicircle radius in a Nyquist plot, the smaller of the carrier transfer resistance. From Fig. 10b, the carrier transfer resistance of CDs@BiOCOOH is markedly smaller than that of single BiOCOOH. After the reaction of CDs@BiOCOOH with NaBH4, a further reduced resistance is presented for the obtained CDs@(BiO)2CO3. To confirm it, we prepared the sample of (BiO)2CO3 (XRD pattern and absorption spectrum shown in Fig. S7) by direct reaction between BiOCOOH and NaBH4 similar with the preparation of CDs@(BiO)2CO3. As shown in Fig. 10b, the carrier transfer resistance of (BiO)2CO3 is very close to that of BiOCOOH. Accordingly, the in situ transformation of BiOCOOH to (BiO)2CO3 on the surface of CDs could improve their interfaces and so minishes the carrier transfer resistance. The higher photocurrent responses from CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 indicate their smaller carrier transfer resistance as well and more photoinduced charges migrated to their surface under visible light (Fig. 10c), thus in favor of the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. Besides, we found that the photodegradation rates of (BiO)2CO3 for dyes were distinctly lower than those of CDs@BiOCOOH and CDs@(BiO)2CO3 even though it had a broad and strong visible-light harvesting ability as well (Figs. S8–S10). Abundant structure defects in (BiO)2CO3 could modify its band gaps, giving rise to visible light absorption. However, they also endow more recombination traps for carrier migration and lead to less photoinduced charges at the surface (Fig. 10c). All of above results verify that the CDs can propel charge separation and transfer. According to recent achievements reported by Huang’s group [36–38], the combination of CDs with (BiO)2CO3 or BiOCOOH may induce the asymmetric charge distribution, rendering the formation of a polarization electric field with the ability to promote separation and transfer of photogenerated charges.

Since the stability of catalysts is essential in real use. Thereby, we operated the cycling tests for the RhB degradation with CDs@(BiO)2CO3 under visible light irradiation. From Fig. 11 a, no any deactivation of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 is observed after four consecutive experiments, illuminating that CDs@(BiO)2CO3 is highly stable during the catalytic reaction under visible light irradiation. Moreover, Fig. 11b displays the effects of light intensities on photodegradation rates of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for RhB dyes. Apparently, the photodegradation rates of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 increase with the visible light source intensity. One can observe a linear dependence of the photodegradation rate on the light intensity, demonstrating that the photocatalytic reaction activity of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for dye degradation has highly positive correlation with the number of absorbed photons [39]. For our presented photocatalyst, hence its efficiency of pollutant degradation can be enhanced by increasing light intensity as well.

We have presented a facile strategy to obtain CDs@(BiO)2CO3 with highly efficient use of sunlight in a large scale. We demonstrated that CDs@(BiO)2CO3 can be produced at room temperature by taking advantage of the reaction of CDs@BiOCOOH with NaBH4. As-prepared CDs@(BiO)2CO3 contains abundant structural defects and extends its response to visible light, leading to an enhanced activity for the degradation of organic pollutants under visible light irradiation. Using our developed technique for the quantification and the kinetics evaluation of the intrinsic reactive species produced in photocatalystic reaction, we found that the maximal production velocity of CDs@(BiO)2CO3 for reactive •OH radicals is 7.6 and 2.1 times higher than that of BiOCOOH and CDs@BiOCOOH, respectively. This could be attributed to its markedly enhanced degradation rates for model dyes. Moreover, CDs in the prepared heterostructures not only act as a photosensitizer, but also facilitate the charge separation and transfer through interface effects. Our presented CDs@(BiO)2CO3 exhibits the superior photocatalytic stability and highly positive correlation with the light intensity as well, suggesting that it has extremely high practical value for environmental remediation using solar energy.

Zhaoqi Li: Methodology, Investigation, Investigation. Qing Chang: Writing - original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Chaorui Xue: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Huiqi Wang: Resources, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Ning Li: Validation, Writing - original draft. Hantao Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) for of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1510125, 51502270, 21703209).
